9237 Brown Road
Elk Grove, CA95624
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-176
Dear Mr. Stanger:
This letter is-in response to your request for assistance regarding the ,.revolving door,,
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the1'Act").1 Because you are requesting general
information on the operation of the law,. and do not inquire about u nuoo*ly res'tricted post-
employment business plan, we are providing you withlnformur usirtu*".,' pi"ur" bear in mind
also that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. we offer no opirrio, on the
application, if any, of such provisions as Government Code Section 1090, or or|", porr-
govemment employment laws such as Public Contract Code Section l04ll. We urge you to
consult with your agency's counsel or the Attorney General's office on these provisions,
particularly regarding Public contract Code Section l04l l.
QtrEsTroNs
l' what restrictions does the Act impose on your doing business with the Department of
Health Care Services, or other state ager"ie, after you leave state service?
2' What restrictions does the Act place on consulting work you would perform if, after
leaving state service, you start your own consulting firm serving priiate companies
that wish to do business with the state?
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code sections g1000 through gl0l4. All statutory
references are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
Commission are contained in sections I8l l0 through l8gg7 of ritle 2 of the California code otRegulations. All
regulations may be found in Title 2, Division 6 of the California code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
- Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the irrunity provfued by u, opiniun n. formal written
advice. (Section 831 t4; regulation 1g329(c)43), copy enclosed).


File No. I-09-176
Page No. 2
3' Does the Act bar you from discussing future job opportunities in the private sector
while still employed by the State?
CONCLUSIONS
we review in general terms the.Act's limitations applicable to your post-retirement
employment plans, and further advise that the Act does ."rt l"t you, gou"-ir"ntal decision-
making powers in matters directly relating to any p".ro, *itt whom"you ur" ,"gotiuting, or have
any rurangement concerning, prospective employment.
F'ACTS
You are Director of the Fiscal Intermediary - Medicaid Management Information System
("FI-MMIS") Project i, F-"-Department of Health care Services ("DHCS,,), a department within
the California Health and Human service_s Agency. nr-rtartars will seek a replacement contract
for a service provider to run the State's Medital .rui*r p.ocessing function, and is also seeking
oversight contractors for the large FI contract. As Director of the Ft-v1ils project, you have
overall responsibility and decisionmaking authority in these proceedings.
Prior to appointment to your current position, you were on the DHCS Executive Staff as
chief of the Fiscal Intermediary and Contracts oversight ("FICoD") Division. In that position
you managed Branches that obtained t..ar.: and techiology contracts and managed the contracts
for the two state FIs (currently EDS and Delta), as well ur itt "r 
large contracir. r" this capacity,
you made governmental decisions on contracts with private firms.
You are contemplating retirement from state service, and will seek employment in the
private sector' The two options you are considering *" "-ployrnent with u.J^pury ttut
provides services to state departments, or establishing you, o*r, consulting practice, which
would focus on work with private-sector companies tt ut ao business with or wish to do business
with the state. Specificalry, you would providi the following services:
o Strategic planning,
o Development of marketing and business plans,
o Reviewing, editing and assisting in deveiopment of proposals responsive to bid requests,
o Research into potential contract opportunities,
o Business development services,
o Training on State administrative and legislative processes and requirements,
o Technical consulting services in the area of health care.


File No. I-09-176
Page No. 3
ANALYSIS
Public officials are subject to three types of post-govemmental restrictions under the Act.
A one-year "revolving door" restriction bari certainstateiemployees from communicating, for
compensation, with their former agencies for the purpose or inRuencing certain administrative or
legislative action. (Section g7406, Regulation tn+e U
A permanent ban prohibits a former state employ": flgrn "switching sides,, to participate,
for compensation, in a proceeding involving the state oi califomia if the proceeding is one in
which the former state employee participatJd while employed by the state. (secrions g7401-
87 4O2, Regulation 187 41.1.)
Finally, a restriction on influencing prospective employment prohibits a public employee
from participating in, or using his or her officiaiposition to i#tu.n.o u gou"*_ental decision
directly relating to any person with whom !e or_she is negotiating or has an arrangement
conceming prospective employment. (Section gT4oT,RJgulatioi 1g747.)
The One-Year ..Revolving Door', Restriction
A. General.Application of the One-year Ban.
section 87406 prohibits specified officials from acting as agents or attomeys or otherwise
representing, for compensation, "any other person, by making *vlormal o, inro*ut
appearance, or by making any oral or written "o*-,-i"rtion, beiore *y.rut" uaministrative
agency' or officer or employee thereof," for one year after the official tjt ilr" ug"ncy,s
employment"if the appearance or communicatiin i, -aa" jo, ;i;;;;;;) iJinltr"nrirg
administrative or legislative action, or influencing any oiiron or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation oi o plr*ft, license, grant, or contract, or the
sale or purchase of goods or property.', (Emphasi, uOa"A.i
Regulation 187 46.2(a) further provides:
"(a) For purposes of Govemment Code Secti on 87406,a formal or inforriral
appearance or oral or written communication is for the purpose of influencing if it
is made for the principar purpose of supporting, promoting, irflu"n"ing,
modifying, opposing, delaying, or advincing ilie action oiproceedid:*
appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by
telephone or in person, corresponding with in writing or by electronic -
transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or-sending any communication.,,
we assume from your employment history that you were required to file a Form 700
"Statement of Economic Interests;' ai a "designated employee" under ttre agerrcy,s conflict of
interest code, and you are therefore subject tothe Act's one-year ban.


File No. l-09-176
Page No.4
Regulation 18746.1(b) outlines the circumstances when the prohibitions of the one-year
ban will apply. Under this regulation, an official covered by the orr"-yr* uan is prohibited from
making an appearance or communication if all of the following apply:
"(1) The official has left his or her state office or employment, which means he or
she has either permanently left state service or is on a leaue of absence.
"(2) The appearance or communication is made within 12 months after leaving
state office or employment.
"(3) The public official is compensated, or promised compensation, for the
appearance or communication. However, a payment made for necessary travel,
meals, and accommodations received directiyin connection with voluntary
services is not prohibited or limited by this slction.
"(4) The appearance or communication is made on behalf of any person as an
agent, attomey, or representative of that person. An appearance or communication
made by a public official solely to represent his or hei personal interests, as
defined in2 cal.code Regs., section lg7oz.4, subdivision (bxr), is not
prohibited or limited by this section.
"(5) The appearance or communication is made for the purpose of influencing, as
defined in2 CaL Code Regs. Section 18746.2,any legisiative or administrative
action, or anl discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or pur"t ur. of g6ods
or property.
"(A) Services performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of
an existing p-ermit, license, grant, contract, or sale agreement may b" excluded
from the prohibitions of this regulation, provided the services do not involve the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or
proceedings....
"(6) The appearance or corlmunication is made before any officer or employee of
any of the following:
"(A) Any state administrative agency that the public official worked for or
represented during the 12 months before leaving state office or employment . . ..
"(B) Any state administrative agency which budget, personnel, *d ot[.,
operations are subject to the direction and control of any agency described in
subdivision (bX6)(A). . ..
"(C) Any state administrative agency subject to the direction and control of the
Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor,s office
during the 12 months before leaving state office Lr employment.,'
Communications.restricted by the one-year ban include any formal or informal
appearance or oral or written communication made to influence legislative or administrative
action or any action on a proceeding. (Section 87406(d)(l).) TheJe communications include,
but are not limited to, conversing directly or by telephone, corresponding by writing or e-mail,


File No. I-09-176
Page No. 5
attending a meeting, and delivering orsending any communication. (Regulation rc7a6.2(a).)
A communication is considered to be for the purpose of influencing legiiative or administrative
action "if it is made for the principal purpose of supportirg, pro*oting, influencing, modifying,
opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or procJedingl'3 (Regula'tion tg746.i(a).)
Certain communications are not restricted under the one-year ban. A communication is
not subject to the one-year ban when the former official:
"(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public
event for educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject matter
does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding;
"(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event;
"(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or
"(4) communicares with the press." (Regulation rg746.2(b)(r)-(4).)
Thus, the one-year ban would not prevent you from requesting information generally
available to the public about agency business from your former employer or other state agencies.
Nor would it prohibit you from attending informational meetings regarding existing laws,
regulations, or policies, as long as you do not attempt to influeice legislati"ve or administrative
actions by your former agency. Social conversations with employeei of your former agency that
are not aimed at influencing its administrative or legislative actions *" not prohibited i'y tfrl Uan.
(Tobias Advice Letter, No. 4-96-089.) (Section SZZOO(aXt).)
Whether a particular meeting or conversation is for the purpose of influencing
administrative or legislative action or a specific proceeding (as defined by Regul afio; ft746.2)
depends on the facts of each case. For instance,lf un ex-eirployee attends , p"uuti" meeting with
many other persons, where there are many topics on the ug"rAu, it may be reasonable to infer
that the ex-employee's attendance is not for the purpose oT irflu"nrinj trre u!"n.y'. action.
conversely, where there is a small meeting to diicuis a particular adriinistraiive or legislative
action, or other specific_action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods o, p.opiiy
3 "kgislative action" is defined at Section 82037 to mean "the drafting, introduction, consideration,
modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the
Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member or
employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. ;Legislative action', also means the action of the
Governor in approving or vetoing any bill." section g2Di(u) piovides that ."Administrative action, means the
proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment, enaciment,.or defeat by any state agency of any rule,
regulation, or other action in any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislatir. p.o.".Jirg,;hi.l, shall include any
proceeding governed by chapter 3.5 (commencing with Section i t:+o) of part I of Division 3 of Title 2.


File No. I-09-176
Page No. 6
(section 87406(d)), it may more readily be inferred that the former employee,s presence at the
meeting is intended to influence agency action. (RamirezAdvice Letter, N". alqg-300.);" --
Similarly, as noted above, Regulation 18746.1(b)(5XA) provides that performing services
to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an exisiing permit, licinse, grant,
contract' or sale agreement are not considered appearances or communications prohibited under
Section 87406, so long as you do not influence oiher administrative or legislative action, or the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, 6r contract, or the sale or
purchase of goods or property.
Accordingly, for purposes of the one-year ban under Section B7406,the Act does not
prevent you from accepting post-governmental employment in the private sector, but for twelve
months after you leave state employment, you maynot make *y upp"**"" b.fo." your former
employer for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing any
action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendmerrt, a**ding, or revocation of a permit,
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods o. prop".'rty
The Permanent Ban on,,switching Sides,'
The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides,, and
participating, for compensation, in any specifi" p.o"."dirg involving the stail of Califomia or
assisting others in the proceeding if the proceeding is oneln which tle former state employee
participated while employed by the state. (See Sections 87401-87402; Regularion 1g741.1.)
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban applicable to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which you participated while employed as a state administrative official.
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' *"un, any proceeding, application, request for a
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigiiion, ctrarge, accusation,
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any cotit or state
administrative agency . . .." (section 87400(c).) ettorricial ias "participatJ,i in u proceeding if
he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval,
disapproval, formal written recommendation, randering advice on a subst-antial basis,
investigation, or use of confidentiar information . . .." fsection g7400(d).)
The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior pro"."dirg in which the official f,.Jp-*j.rp"i.a.
A "new" proceeding nol subject to the p"t-an".rt ban typlally involves different parties, subject
matter, or circumstances that differ from those involved in prwious proceedings. (Risf Advice
Letter, No. 4-04-187; also see Donovar Advice Letter, No. l-o:-ttd.; New ";r;r;;;;i;iiil"
employee's former agency' in which the former employee did not participate, are considered new
proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A l'new" contract is one based on new
consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same parties. (FerberAdvice Letter, No. I-
a Further information is provided in the enclosed fact sheet prepared by the Commission to address
common questions associated with post-employment restrictions.


File No. I-09-176
Page No.7
99-104; Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In addition, the application, drafting, and the
award of a contract, lissnss, or approval is considered a proceeding-separate from theinonitoring
and performance of the contract, license, or approval. (Anderson,iupro; BlonienAdvice LetterJ
No. 4-89-463.)
While we have outlined the general provisions of the permanent ban for your review, you
have not advised us in detail of your participation in specific |roceedings while in state service,
nor of the potential relationship between any such proceedingi and matLrs on which you will be
employed by a private-sector client. To evaluate application of th" permanent ban to services
you may lawfully offer a private-sector client, you will need to determine whether any of the
services you may provide to a new employer involve a proceeding in which you partilipated
while employed by the State. (Regulation l874l.l(a)t+).1 rf you need assisiance in evaluating
private-sector services_ relating to a specific proceeding in which you may have previously
participated, you should seek further advice when you can providl us with the relevant facts.
The Ban Against Influencing Prospective Employment
The Act's final restriction relating to post-govemmental employment in the ban against
influencing prospective employment, found at Section g7407:
"No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her official
position to influence, any governmental decision diiectly relating to any person
with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement con-erning,
prospective employment.',
The term "public official" is defined, in part, in Section SZO4Bas ". . . every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency, but does not include
judges and court commissioners in the judicial branch of govemment. . ..,, DHCS is a state
administrative agency, as defined in Section 87400(a), *d as a retiring Director of its FI-MMIS
Project, you would be subject to this ban.
"A public official is 'negotiating' employment when he or she interviews or discusses an
offer of employment with an [potential] employer or his or her agent." (Regulation fi7a1@)(l).)
The Commission has construed the scheduling, conduct, and foll-ow-rpio ir interview as one
continuous process falling under the definition of "negotiating" employm ent. (Bonner Advice
Letter, No' I-98-287.) However, the mere act of sending a resume or application to a potential
employer has not been considered "negotiating." Similarly, entertaining- informal inquiries about
your future plans and accepting expressions of general interest in discus-sing r.ptoy-.ni
opportunities at some point in the future is not considered "negotiatin g." U;) ,.A public official
has an 'arrangement' concerning prospective employment when rr" oirt e u.."pt. an employer,s
offer of employment. " (Regulat ion I 87 47 (c)(2).)


File No. l-09-176
Page No. 8
We hope that this discussion of the Act's post-employment restrictions will assist you in
framing and pursuing your post-separation plans. If you have other questions in this area, please
contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

Senior Counsel, Legal Division

The Permanent Ban on,,switching Sides,'
The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides,, and
participating, for compensation, in any specifi" p.o"."dirg involving the stail of Califomia or
assisting others in the proceeding if the proceeding is oneln which tle former state employee
participated while employed by the state. (See Sections 87401-87402; Regularion 1g741.1.)
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban applicable to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which you participated while employed as a state administrative official.
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' *"un, any proceeding, application, request for a
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigiiion, ctrarge, accusation,
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any cotit or state
administrative agency . . .." (section 87400(c).) ettorricial ias "participatJ,i in u proceeding if
he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval,
disapproval, formal written recommendation, randering advice on a subst-antial basis,
investigation, or use of confidentiar information . . .." fsection g7400(d).)
The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior pro"."dirg in which the official f,.Jp-*j.rp"i.a.
A "new" proceeding nol subject to the p"t-an".rt ban typlally involves different parties, subject
matter, or circumstances that differ from those involved in prwious proceedings. (Risf Advice
Letter, No. 4-04-187; also see Donovar Advice Letter, No. l-o:-ttd.; New ";r;r;;;;i;iiil"
employee's former agency' in which the former employee did not participate, are considered new
proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A l'new" contract is one based on new
consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same parties. (FerberAdvice Letter, No. I-
a Further information is provided in the enclosed fact sheet prepared by the Commission to address
common questions associated with post-employment restrictions.
99-104; Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In addition, the application, drafting, and the
award of a contract, lissnss, or approval is considered a proceeding-separate from theinonitoring
and performance of the contract, license, or approval. (Anderson,iupro; BlonienAdvice LetterJ
No. 4-89-463.)
While we have outlined the general provisions of the permanent ban for your review, you
have not advised us in detail of your participation in specific |roceedings while in state service,
nor of the potential relationship between any such proceedingi and matLrs on which you will be
employed by a private-sector client. To evaluate application of th" permanent ban to services
you may lawfully offer a private-sector client, you will need to determine whether any of the
services you may provide to a new employer involve a proceeding in which you partilipated
while employed by the State. (Regulation l874l.l(a)t+).1 rf you need assisiance in evaluating
private-sector services_ relating to a specific proceeding in which you may have previously
participated, you should seek further advice when you can providl us with the relevant facts.
The Ban Against Influencing Prospective Employment
The Act's final restriction relating to post-govemmental employment in the ban against
influencing prospective employment, found at Section g7407:
"No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her official
position to influence, any governmental decision diiectly relating to any person
with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement con-erning,
prospective employment.',
The term "public official" is defined, in part, in Section SZO4Bas ". . . every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency, but does not include
judges and court commissioners in the judicial branch of govemment. . ..,, DHCS is a state
administrative agency, as defined in Section 87400(a), *d as a retiring Director of its FI-MMIS
Project, you would be subject to this ban.
"A public official is 'negotiating' employment when he or she interviews or discusses an
offer of employment with an [potential] employer or his or her agent." (Regulation fi7a1@)(l).)
The Commission has construed the scheduling, conduct, and foll-ow-rpio ir interview as one
continuous process falling under the definition of "negotiating" employm ent. (Bonner Advice
Letter, No' I-98-287.) However, the mere act of sending a resume or application to a potential
employer has not been considered "negotiating." Similarly, entertaining- informal inquiries about
your future plans and accepting expressions of general interest in discus-sing r.ptoy-.ni
opportunities at some point in the future is not considered "negotiatin g." U;) ,.A public official
has an 'arrangement' concerning prospective employment when rr" oirt e u.."pt. an employer,s
offer of employment. " (Regulat ion I 87 47 (c)(2).)


File No. l-09-176
We hope that this discussion of the Act's post-employment restrictions will assist you in
framing and pursuing your post-separation plans. If you have other questions in this area, please
contact me at (916) 322-5660.
